title: EGR Flow Malfunction
shortDescription: The exhaust gas recirculation (EGR) system flow is not functioning as expected.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the EGR system flow is outside the expected parameters. The EGR system recirculates a portion of exhaust gases back into the intake manifold to reduce combustion temperatures and lower NOx emissions. This code indicates a general malfunction in the EGR flow, which could be caused by a clogged EGR passage, faulty EGR valve, or issues with the EGR control solenoid.
safetyImpact: May cause increased emissions and potential engine performance issues, but generally safe to drive short distances.

causes.cause: Clogged EGR passages or tubes with carbon buildup
Faulty EGR valve stuck open or closed
Defective EGR vacuum solenoid or control solenoid
Vacuum supply line leak or disconnection

symptoms: Engine knocking or pinging under load

fixSteps.title: Inspect EGR Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the EGR valve and inspect it for carbon buildup and proper movement. Check if the valve diaphragm holds vacuum.

fixSteps.title: Clean EGR Passages
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the EGR valve and clean the exhaust passages with carburetor cleaner and a wire brush. Clear any carbon deposits blocking the flow.

fixSteps.title: Test EGR Solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Use a multimeter to check the EGR control solenoid resistance and verify it activates when commanded by the ECM using a scan tool.

fixSteps.title: Replace Faulty Components
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the EGR valve, solenoid, or vacuum lines as needed. Clear codes and test drive to verify the repair.

faq.q: Can I drive with a P0400 code?
faq.a: Yes, you can usually drive short distances, but prolonged driving may increase emissions and cause engine knocking that could lead to engine damage.

faq.q: What does the EGR system do?
faq.a: The EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) system redirects a small amount of exhaust gas back into the intake to lower combustion temperatures and reduce harmful NOx emissions.

faq.q: How often does the EGR valve need to be cleaned?
faq.a: EGR valves can accumulate carbon over time. Cleaning every 50,000 miles or when symptoms appear is a good preventive practice.

title: EGR Flow Insufficient
shortDescription: The EGR system flow is detected as insufficient by the ECM.
fullDescription: The ECM has determined that the EGR system is not flowing enough exhaust gas when commanded. This is often caused by carbon buildup restricting the EGR passages or a stuck-closed EGR valve. The DPFE (Differential Pressure Feedback EGR) sensor or its hoses may also be at fault, providing incorrect flow readings to the ECM.
safetyImpact: Increased NOx emissions and possible engine knock under load, but generally safe to drive to a repair facility.

causes.cause: Carbon buildup in EGR passages restricting flow
Faulty DPFE sensor or EGR pressure feedback sensor
EGR valve stuck closed or not opening fully
Clogged or cracked DPFE sensor hoses

symptoms: Engine knock or ping during acceleration
Failed emissions test for high NOx

fixSteps.title: Test DPFE Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Check the DPFE sensor voltage readings with the engine running and EGR commanded on. Compare to manufacturer specifications.

fixSteps.title: Inspect DPFE Hoses
fixSteps.instruction: Check the rubber hoses connecting the DPFE sensor to the EGR tube for cracks, clogs, or disconnections.

fixSteps.title: Clean EGR Valve and Passages
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the EGR valve and clean it along with the intake passages using appropriate solvent to remove carbon deposits.

fixSteps.title: Replace DPFE Sensor if Faulty
fixSteps.instruction: If the DPFE sensor readings are out of range, replace it and clear codes. Test drive to confirm repair.

faq.q: What is a DPFE sensor?
faq.a: The Differential Pressure Feedback EGR sensor measures the pressure difference across the EGR orifice to determine exhaust gas flow rate and report it to the ECM.

faq.q: Is P0401 a common code on Ford vehicles?
faq.a: Yes, P0401 is very common on Ford vehicles, often caused by a failed DPFE sensor, which is an inexpensive and easy repair.

faq.q: Will P0401 cause my car to fail emissions?
faq.a: Yes, this code will cause your vehicle to fail an OBD-II emissions test since it indicates the EGR system is not operating properly.

title: EGR Flow Excessive
shortDescription: The ECM has detected excessive exhaust gas recirculation flow.
fullDescription: The ECM has determined that the EGR valve is allowing too much exhaust gas into the intake manifold. Excessive EGR flow dilutes the air-fuel mixture excessively, causing rough running, stalling, and poor performance. This is typically caused by an EGR valve stuck open, a faulty EGR pressure sensor reading incorrectly, or a short in the EGR solenoid circuit.
safetyImpact: Can cause stalling at idle or during deceleration, which may be hazardous in traffic situations.

causes.cause: EGR valve stuck in the open position
Faulty DPFE or EGR pressure feedback sensor
EGR solenoid stuck open or shorted circuit
Vacuum line routing error after servicing

symptoms: Engine stalling, especially at idle or deceleration

fixSteps.title: Inspect EGR Valve Operation
fixSteps.instruction: With the engine idling, tap on the EGR valve. If the engine stumbles or stalls, the valve is stuck open. Check for carbon preventing full closure.

fixSteps.title: Test Pressure Feedback Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Check the DPFE sensor output voltage with the engine off and at idle. If readings are abnormally high, the sensor is likely faulty.

fixSteps.title: Check EGR Solenoid Circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Test the EGR solenoid for proper resistance and check the wiring harness for shorts or damage.

fixSteps.title: Replace Faulty Components
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the stuck EGR valve or faulty sensor. Ensure proper gasket seating on reinstallation. Clear codes and verify repair.

faq.q: Can excessive EGR flow damage my engine?
faq.a: While it won't cause immediate damage, prolonged operation with excessive EGR flow can lead to carbon buildup in the intake and combustion chambers.

faq.q: Why does my car stall with P0402?
faq.a: Too much exhaust gas displaces fresh air in the intake, leaning out the combustible mixture to the point where the engine cannot sustain idle.

faq.q: Can I just disconnect the EGR valve?
faq.a: Disconnecting the EGR valve is not recommended as it will cause a check engine light and emissions failure, and may increase combustion temperatures leading to engine knock.

title: EGR Circuit Malfunction
shortDescription: The EGR valve control circuit has an electrical malfunction.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected an electrical fault in the EGR valve control circuit. This code relates to the electrical side of the EGR system rather than mechanical flow issues. The problem could be in the EGR valve solenoid itself, the wiring between the ECM and the EGR valve, or a connector issue. On vehicles with electronic EGR valves, the internal motor or position sensor may have failed.
safetyImpact: Emissions will increase and the engine may run poorly, but generally safe to drive to a repair facility.

causes.cause: Faulty EGR valve solenoid or internal motor
Corroded or damaged wiring to the EGR valve
Poor electrical connector contact at the EGR valve
Failed ECM EGR driver circuit (rare)

fixSteps.title: Check EGR Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the electrical connector at the EGR valve for corrosion, bent pins, or moisture. Clean contacts with electrical contact cleaner.

fixSteps.title: Test EGR Solenoid Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the resistance of the EGR valve solenoid coil with a multimeter. Compare to manufacturer specifications (typically 20-40 ohms).

fixSteps.title: Inspect Wiring Harness
fixSteps.instruction: Check the wiring from the ECM to the EGR valve for breaks, chafing, or shorts. Wiggle test the harness while monitoring resistance.

fixSteps.title: Replace EGR Valve
fixSteps.instruction: If the solenoid resistance is out of spec, replace the EGR valve. Clear the code and verify proper operation.

faq.q: What is the difference between P0400 and P0403?
faq.a: P0400 indicates a general EGR flow problem, while P0403 specifically points to an electrical fault in the EGR control circuit.

faq.q: Can a bad ground cause P0403?
faq.a: Yes, a poor ground connection for the EGR solenoid circuit can cause erratic voltage readings that trigger this code.

faq.q: How do I know if my EGR valve is electronic or vacuum-operated?
faq.a: Most vehicles from 2000 onward use electronic EGR valves. Check your repair manual or look for an electrical connector on the valve versus a vacuum hose.

title: EGR Circuit Range/Performance
shortDescription: The EGR valve position or performance is outside the expected range.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the EGR valve is not performing within its expected range. This means the actual valve position does not match the commanded position. The EGR valve may be responding too slowly, sticking intermittently, or the position sensor inside the valve may be providing inaccurate feedback to the ECM.
safetyImpact: May cause intermittent rough running or stalling. Drive cautiously, especially in heavy traffic.

causes.cause: Carbon buildup causing the EGR valve to stick
Worn or faulty EGR valve position sensor
EGR valve pintle worn or damaged
Intermittent wiring issue in the EGR circuit

fixSteps.title: Monitor EGR Valve with Scan Tool
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to command the EGR valve through its range and observe the position feedback. Look for sticking or erratic movement.
tools: OBD-II scan tool with live data

fixSteps.title: Clean the EGR Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and thoroughly clean the EGR valve with carburetor cleaner, paying attention to the pintle and seat area.

fixSteps.title: Test Position Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: With the valve removed, manually move the pintle and check for smooth, linear voltage change from the position sensor.

fixSteps.title: Replace EGR Valve if Needed
fixSteps.instruction: If cleaning does not resolve the sticking or sensor issue, replace the EGR valve assembly. Clear codes and verify.

faq.q: Can cleaning the EGR valve fix P0404?
faq.a: Yes, in many cases cleaning the EGR valve to remove carbon deposits will restore proper operation and resolve this code.

faq.q: Is P0404 a common code on GM vehicles?
faq.a: Yes, P0404 is particularly common on GM vehicles and is often resolved by replacing or cleaning the EGR valve.

faq.q: What happens if I ignore P0404?
faq.a: Ignoring this code may lead to increased emissions, worsening drivability issues, and potential catalytic converter damage from improper combustion.

title: EGR Sensor A Low
shortDescription: The EGR valve position sensor A circuit is reading below the expected voltage range.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the EGR position sensor A signal voltage is lower than the minimum expected threshold. This typically indicates a short to ground in the sensor circuit, a failed EGR position sensor, or a wiring issue. The ECM uses this sensor to verify the EGR valve is responding correctly to commanded positions.
safetyImpact: The ECM may disable EGR operation, leading to increased emissions. Drivability may be slightly affected.

causes.cause: Faulty EGR valve position sensor
Short to ground in the sensor signal wire
Corroded or damaged connector at the EGR valve
Open circuit in the sensor reference voltage wire

fixSteps.title: Check Sensor Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: With the key on and engine off, check the EGR position sensor voltage. It should read approximately 0.5-1.0V at the closed position.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Connector and Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check the EGR valve connector for corrosion and verify the reference voltage (5V) and ground are present at the connector.

fixSteps.title: Check for Short to Ground
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the EGR valve and check the signal wire for continuity to ground. There should be no continuity.

fixSteps.title: Replace EGR Valve or Repair Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: If the sensor is faulty, replace the EGR valve. If a wiring issue is found, repair the damaged wire. Clear codes and verify.

faq.q: What is EGR Sensor A?
faq.a: EGR Sensor A is the primary position feedback sensor on the EGR valve that tells the ECM how far the valve is open.

faq.q: Can a bad connection cause P0405?
faq.a: Yes, a corroded or loose connector can cause the signal voltage to drop below the expected range, triggering this code.

faq.q: Is the EGR position sensor replaceable separately?
faq.a: On most vehicles, the position sensor is integrated into the EGR valve and the entire valve assembly must be replaced.

title: Secondary Air System Malfunction
shortDescription: The secondary air injection system is not functioning properly.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected a malfunction in the secondary air injection (AIR) system. This system pumps fresh air into the exhaust manifold during cold starts to help the catalytic converter heat up faster and reduce cold-start emissions. The problem could be a failed air pump, a stuck check valve, a faulty air switching valve, or issues with the system's control circuit.
safetyImpact: Increased cold-start emissions but generally safe to drive. The catalytic converter may take longer to reach operating temperature.

causes.cause: Failed secondary air injection pump
Stuck or leaking check valve in the air injection system
Faulty air switching solenoid or relay
Cracked or disconnected air injection hoses

symptoms: Unusual noise from the air pump area during cold start
Possible sulfur smell from exhaust during cold start

fixSteps.title: Listen for Air Pump
fixSteps.instruction: During a cold start, listen for the secondary air pump running (typically runs for 30-90 seconds). No sound may indicate a failed pump or relay.

fixSteps.title: Test Air Pump Relay and Fuse
fixSteps.instruction: Check the secondary air pump fuse and relay. Swap the relay with a known good one to test. Verify power and ground at the pump connector.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Check Valves
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and inspect the one-way check valves in the air injection system. They should allow air to flow toward the exhaust only.

fixSteps.title: Replace Failed Components
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the air pump, check valve, or control solenoid as needed. Check all hoses for cracks. Clear codes and cold-start verify.

faq.q: What does the secondary air injection system do?
faq.a: It pumps fresh air into the exhaust during cold starts to help the catalytic converter reach operating temperature faster, reducing emissions.

faq.q: Is P0410 common on certain vehicles?
faq.a: Yes, P0410 is especially common on VW, Audi, and BMW vehicles, often due to failed check valves or moisture-damaged air pumps.

faq.q: Can I drive without the secondary air pump?
faq.a: Yes, the vehicle will run fine but will produce higher cold-start emissions and will fail an emissions test.

title: Secondary Air Incorrect Flow
shortDescription: The secondary air injection system flow is detected as incorrect.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the secondary air injection system is not delivering the correct amount of airflow to the exhaust. The system is running but not achieving the expected change in oxygen sensor readings. This is commonly caused by a stuck check valve preventing air from reaching the exhaust, air leaks in the system, or a weak air pump that cannot produce sufficient pressure.
safetyImpact: Primarily an emissions concern. Safe to drive but should be repaired to pass emissions testing.

causes.cause: Check valve stuck closed preventing airflow to exhaust
Leaking or disconnected air injection hoses
Weak air pump not producing adequate pressure
Carbon buildup blocking air injection ports in exhaust manifold

symptoms: Possible rough running during cold start

fixSteps.title: Inspect Hoses and Connections
fixSteps.instruction: Check all secondary air injection hoses for cracks, leaks, or disconnections. Look for melted or heat-damaged hoses near the exhaust manifold.

fixSteps.title: Test Check Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the check valve and verify air can flow in one direction only. A stuck or corroded check valve is a common failure point.

fixSteps.title: Verify Air Pump Output
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the air pump output hose and start the engine cold. Feel for strong airflow from the pump during the first 60 seconds.

fixSteps.title: Clear Exhaust Ports and Replace Parts
fixSteps.instruction: If exhaust ports are blocked, clear them. Replace any failed check valves, hoses, or the air pump. Clear codes and verify with a cold start.

faq.q: What is the difference between P0410 and P0411?
faq.a: P0410 indicates a general system malfunction, while P0411 specifically means the system is running but not delivering the correct amount of airflow.

faq.q: Can moisture damage the secondary air pump?
faq.a: Yes, moisture entering through failed check valves can rust and seize the air pump impeller, which is a common failure mode.

faq.q: Will P0411 cause any drivability issues?
faq.a: P0411 typically does not cause noticeable drivability issues since the system only operates briefly during cold starts.

title: Catalyst Efficiency Below Threshold
shortDescription: The catalytic converter on bank 1 is operating below the efficiency threshold.
fullDescription: The ECM has determined that the bank 1 catalytic converter is not efficiently converting exhaust pollutants. The ECM monitors catalyst efficiency by comparing the upstream (pre-cat) and downstream (post-cat) oxygen sensor signals. When the downstream sensor activity closely mirrors the upstream sensor, it indicates the catalyst is no longer effectively storing and releasing oxygen. This is one of the most common OBD-II codes.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive but emissions are significantly increased. Prolonged driving may damage other emissions components.

causes.cause: Worn or deteriorated catalytic converter
Failed downstream oxygen sensor providing incorrect readings
Engine misfire contaminating the catalytic converter
Exhaust leak before the downstream oxygen sensor
Rich or lean fuel condition damaging the catalyst over time

symptoms: Possible sulfur or rotten egg smell from exhaust

fixSteps.title: Check for Other Codes
fixSteps.instruction: Scan for additional codes. Misfire codes, oxygen sensor codes, or fuel system codes should be addressed first as they may be the root cause.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Exhaust for Leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Check the exhaust system for leaks between the engine and the downstream O2 sensor. Leaks can cause false catalyst efficiency readings.

fixSteps.title: Test Oxygen Sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Monitor upstream and downstream O2 sensor signals. The downstream sensor should show a relatively steady voltage if the catalyst is working properly.
tools: OBD-II scan tool with live data

fixSteps.title: Replace Catalytic Converter
fixSteps.instruction: If the catalyst is confirmed failed, replace the catalytic converter with a CARB-compliant unit. Ensure proper fitment and no exhaust leaks.

faq.q: Is P0420 expensive to fix?
faq.a: It can be. If the catalytic converter needs replacement, costs range from $500 to $2,500 depending on the vehicle. However, sometimes the issue is a faulty oxygen sensor, which is much cheaper.

faq.q: Can I drive with P0420?
faq.a: Yes, P0420 is safe to drive with but your vehicle will fail emissions testing and you are producing higher exhaust emissions.

faq.q: Can I use a universal catalytic converter?
faq.a: In some states yes, but California and CARB-compliant states require a specific CARB-approved catalytic converter for your vehicle. Check your local regulations.

title: Warm-Up Catalyst Efficiency Low
shortDescription: The warm-up catalytic converter on bank 1 is below efficiency threshold.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the warm-up catalytic converter (also called the pre-catalyst) on bank 1 is not reaching efficiency standards during the warm-up phase. This close-coupled converter is designed to heat up quickly and begin reducing emissions sooner. This code specifically monitors catalyst performance during the warm-up cycle rather than at full operating temperature.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive. Primarily affects cold-start emissions performance.

causes.cause: Deteriorated warm-up catalytic converter
Faulty upstream or downstream oxygen sensor
Exhaust leak near the warm-up catalyst
Engine running rich during warm-up phase

symptoms: Possible smell from exhaust during warm-up
No significant drivability symptoms in most cases

fixSteps.title: Diagnose Related Issues First
fixSteps.instruction: Check for misfire codes, fuel system codes, and oxygen sensor codes. These issues must be resolved before condemning the catalyst.

fixSteps.title: Monitor O2 Sensor Signals
fixSteps.instruction: During warm-up, monitor the upstream and downstream oxygen sensor voltages. The downstream should show less switching activity than the upstream.

fixSteps.title: Check for Exhaust Leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect exhaust connections near the warm-up catalyst for leaks that could affect sensor readings.

fixSteps.title: Replace Warm-Up Catalyst
fixSteps.instruction: If the catalyst is confirmed degraded, replace the warm-up catalytic converter. Many vehicles have this as part of the exhaust manifold assembly.

faq.q: What is a warm-up catalytic converter?
faq.a: It is a smaller catalytic converter located close to the exhaust manifold that heats up quickly during cold starts to reduce emissions before the main catalyst reaches operating temperature.

faq.q: Is P0421 the same as P0420?
faq.a: They are similar but P0421 specifically monitors catalyst performance during the warm-up cycle, while P0420 monitors overall catalyst efficiency at operating temperature.

faq.q: Can a bad thermostat cause P0421?
faq.a: A thermostat that opens too soon could prevent the engine from reaching optimal temperature quickly, potentially affecting warm-up catalyst monitoring.

title: Catalyst Efficiency Low Bank 2
shortDescription: The catalytic converter on bank 2 is operating below the efficiency threshold.
fullDescription: The ECM has determined that the bank 2 catalytic converter is not efficiently converting exhaust pollutants. This is the same diagnostic as P0420 but for the bank 2 exhaust on V-type or horizontally opposed engines. The ECM compares the upstream and downstream oxygen sensor signals on bank 2 to determine catalyst efficiency.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive but emissions are significantly increased. Should be repaired for emissions compliance.

causes.cause: Worn or failed bank 2 catalytic converter
Failed bank 2 downstream oxygen sensor
Engine misfire on bank 2 cylinders
Exhaust leak on bank 2 before downstream sensor

symptoms: Possible rotten egg smell from exhaust

fixSteps.title: Check for Related Codes
fixSteps.instruction: Scan for misfire or O2 sensor codes on bank 2. Address any underlying issues before replacing the catalyst.

fixSteps.title: Test Bank 2 O2 Sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Monitor bank 2 upstream and downstream oxygen sensor signals. A lazy downstream sensor can falsely trigger this code.
tools: OBD-II scan tool with live data

fixSteps.title: Inspect Bank 2 Exhaust
fixSteps.instruction: Check for exhaust leaks at gaskets, flanges, and flex pipes on the bank 2 side of the exhaust.

fixSteps.title: Replace Bank 2 Catalytic Converter
fixSteps.instruction: If confirmed failed, replace the bank 2 catalytic converter. Ensure proper sensor reinstallation and no exhaust leaks.

faq.q: Which side is bank 2?
faq.a: Bank 2 is the side of the engine that does NOT contain cylinder 1. On most V6 and V8 engines, bank 2 is typically the side closer to the firewall, but this varies by manufacturer.

faq.q: Should I replace both catalytic converters at once?
faq.a: It is not necessary, but if one has failed due to age, the other may be close to failure as well. Consider your vehicle's mileage and budget.

faq.q: Can a P0430 code clear itself?
faq.a: It is unlikely to clear on its own since catalyst degradation is permanent. However, if the root cause was a sensor or exhaust leak, fixing that issue may clear the code.

title: Warm-Up Catalyst Low Bank 2
shortDescription: The warm-up catalytic converter on bank 2 is below efficiency threshold.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the bank 2 warm-up catalytic converter is not performing adequately during the engine warm-up phase. This is the bank 2 equivalent of P0421. The close-coupled catalyst near the bank 2 exhaust manifold is not efficiently converting pollutants during the initial warm-up period.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive. Cold-start emissions are affected but drivability is typically normal.

causes.cause: Deteriorated bank 2 warm-up catalyst
Faulty bank 2 oxygen sensors
Exhaust leak near bank 2 warm-up catalyst
Rich fuel condition on bank 2 during warm-up

symptoms: Possible exhaust odor during cold start

fixSteps.title: Check for Other Fault Codes
fixSteps.instruction: Scan for additional codes on bank 2 that could be causing the catalyst efficiency issue, such as misfire or fuel trim codes.

fixSteps.title: Test Bank 2 O2 Sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Monitor bank 2 oxygen sensor voltages during warm-up. The downstream sensor should show a damped signal compared to the upstream.
tools: OBD-II scan tool with graphing capability

fixSteps.title: Inspect for Exhaust Leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Check the bank 2 exhaust manifold and connections for leaks that could introduce false air to the downstream sensor.

fixSteps.title: Replace Warm-Up Catalyst
fixSteps.instruction: If the catalyst is failed, replace the bank 2 warm-up catalytic converter or exhaust manifold with integrated catalyst.

faq.q: Can both banks fail at the same time?
faq.a: It is possible, especially with high mileage. If you have both P0421 and P0431, both warm-up catalysts may need replacement.

faq.q: Is the warm-up catalyst separate from the main catalyst?
faq.a: On many vehicles yes, the warm-up catalyst is a smaller unit close to the exhaust manifold, while the main catalyst is further downstream.

faq.q: Does P0431 affect fuel economy?
faq.a: P0431 itself does not significantly affect fuel economy since the warm-up catalyst is a passive emissions device.

title: EVAP System Malfunction
shortDescription: A general malfunction has been detected in the evaporative emission control system.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected a general fault in the EVAP system, which is designed to capture fuel vapors from the fuel tank and route them to the engine for combustion rather than releasing them into the atmosphere. This code is a general EVAP code that indicates the system is not functioning as expected but does not pinpoint the specific component. Common causes include a loose gas cap, faulty purge or vent solenoid, or a leak in the EVAP system.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive. This is an emissions-related code that does not affect engine performance or safety.

causes.cause: Loose, damaged, or missing fuel cap
Faulty EVAP purge control solenoid
Faulty EVAP vent control solenoid
Leak in EVAP hoses or charcoal canister

symptoms: Possible fuel odor near the vehicle

fixSteps.title: Check Gas Cap
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the gas cap, inspect the seal for cracks or damage, and reinstall tightly until it clicks. Clear the code and drive for a day.

fixSteps.title: Inspect EVAP Hoses
fixSteps.instruction: Check all EVAP system hoses from the fuel tank to the charcoal canister and purge valve for cracks, disconnections, or damage.

fixSteps.title: Test Purge and Vent Solenoids
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to command the purge and vent solenoids and verify they actuate. Check their resistance with a multimeter.

fixSteps.title: Smoke Test the System
fixSteps.instruction: Perform an EVAP smoke test to identify any leaks in the system. Repair or replace leaking components.

faq.q: Can a loose gas cap cause P0440?
faq.a: Yes, a loose or damaged gas cap is one of the most common causes of P0440 and should always be checked first.

faq.q: What is the EVAP system?
faq.a: The EVAP (Evaporative Emission Control) system captures fuel vapors from the fuel tank and stores them in a charcoal canister, then purges them into the engine for combustion.

faq.q: Will P0440 cause my car to fail emissions?
faq.a: Yes, any check engine light will cause an OBD-II emissions test failure.

title: EVAP Incorrect Purge Flow
shortDescription: The EVAP system purge flow is not within the expected range.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the EVAP system purge flow is incorrect. The purge valve opens to allow stored fuel vapors from the charcoal canister to be drawn into the engine intake manifold. This code indicates the flow is either too much, too little, or not occurring at all. The most common cause is a stuck or faulty purge solenoid valve.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive. No impact on vehicle safety or performance.

causes.cause: Faulty EVAP purge solenoid valve stuck open or closed
Vacuum supply line to purge valve cracked or disconnected
Blocked or kinked EVAP system hoses
Faulty charcoal canister saturated with fuel

symptoms: Possible rough idle if purge valve stuck open

fixSteps.title: Test Purge Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the purge valve and apply vacuum to it with the engine off. It should hold vacuum. With 12V applied, it should release vacuum.

fixSteps.title: Check Vacuum Lines
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect all vacuum hoses connected to the purge valve for cracks, kinks, or disconnections.

fixSteps.title: Monitor with Scan Tool
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to command the purge valve open and closed while monitoring fuel trim changes. A working purge valve will cause a noticeable fuel trim shift.
tools: OBD-II scan tool with bidirectional control

fixSteps.title: Replace Purge Valve
fixSteps.instruction: If the purge valve is faulty, replace it. It is usually accessible on or near the intake manifold. Clear codes and verify.

faq.q: What is the purge valve?
faq.a: The purge valve is an electrically controlled solenoid that opens to allow fuel vapors stored in the charcoal canister to be drawn into the engine intake for combustion.

faq.q: Can a stuck purge valve cause running issues?
faq.a: Yes, a purge valve stuck open can cause a rich condition at idle, leading to rough running, and a stuck-closed valve prevents vapor purging.

faq.q: Where is the purge valve located?
faq.a: The purge valve is typically located on or near the engine intake manifold, connected by hoses to the charcoal canister and intake vacuum.

title: EVAP System Small Leak
shortDescription: A small leak has been detected in the evaporative emission control system.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected a small leak in the EVAP system (typically 0.020 to 0.040 inches in diameter). The ECM tests the EVAP system integrity by sealing the system and monitoring for pressure decay. A small leak allows fuel vapors to escape slowly. This is often caused by a deteriorated gas cap seal, cracked EVAP hose, or a small crack in the charcoal canister.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive. This is a minor emissions leak with no impact on performance or safety.

causes.cause: Deteriorated or cracked gas cap O-ring seal
Small crack or hole in an EVAP hose
Leaking EVAP purge or vent solenoid
Small crack in charcoal canister

fixSteps.title: Inspect and Replace Gas Cap
fixSteps.instruction: Check the gas cap seal for cracks or deterioration. Replace with an OEM gas cap if worn. Clear the code and drive to retest.

fixSteps.title: Visual Inspection of EVAP Lines
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect all EVAP hoses, connections, and the charcoal canister for visible cracks, damage, or loose connections.

fixSteps.title: Perform EVAP Smoke Test
fixSteps.instruction: Use an EVAP smoke machine to pressurize the system with visible smoke. Look for smoke escaping to identify the exact leak location.

fixSteps.title: Repair the Leak
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the cracked hose, connector, or component where the leak was found. Clear codes and allow the EVAP monitor to complete.

faq.q: What is the difference between P0442 and P0455?
faq.a: P0442 indicates a small leak while P0455 indicates a large leak. Small leaks are harder to find but both are emissions-related concerns.

faq.q: Can cold weather cause P0442?
faq.a: Temperature changes can cause slight contraction of seals and hoses that may trigger a small leak detection, especially with marginal components.

faq.q: Do I need a smoke machine to find the leak?
faq.a: A smoke test is the most effective way to find small EVAP leaks. Without one, you may be able to find obvious cracks through visual inspection.

title: EVAP Purge Valve Circuit
shortDescription: The EVAP purge control valve circuit has an electrical malfunction.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected an electrical fault in the EVAP purge control valve circuit. This is different from a flow problem as it specifically indicates a wiring or solenoid electrical issue. The fault could be an open or short circuit in the purge valve solenoid, damaged wiring, or a poor connector connection.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive. EVAP purge will be disabled, increasing emissions but not affecting vehicle safety.

causes.cause: Faulty EVAP purge solenoid coil
Broken or corroded wiring to the purge valve
Poor electrical connection at the purge valve connector
Blown fuse in the purge valve circuit

symptoms: No audible click from purge valve when commanded

fixSteps.title: Check Purge Valve Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the electrical connector at the purge valve for corrosion, bent pins, or damage. Clean with electrical contact cleaner.

fixSteps.title: Test Solenoid Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the purge valve solenoid resistance. It should typically read 20-40 ohms. An open or very low reading indicates a failed solenoid.

fixSteps.title: Check Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Verify power supply and ground at the purge valve connector. Check the wiring harness for breaks or shorts from the ECM to the valve.

fixSteps.title: Replace Purge Valve
fixSteps.instruction: If the solenoid is faulty, replace the purge valve. Clear codes and verify the circuit is functioning properly.

faq.q: How do I know if the purge valve or wiring is the problem?
faq.a: If the solenoid resistance is out of spec, the valve is bad. If resistance is good, check for power supply and ground at the connector to test the wiring.

faq.q: Is the purge valve expensive to replace?
faq.a: No, purge valves are typically $20-80 for the part and are usually easy to access and replace.

faq.q: Can P0443 cause the car to run poorly?
faq.a: Typically no, since the ECM will simply disable EVAP purging when this circuit fault is detected.

title: EVAP Vent System Malfunction
shortDescription: The EVAP vent control system is not functioning properly.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected a problem with the EVAP system vent valve or its circuit. The vent valve is typically located on or near the charcoal canister and is used to seal the EVAP system during leak testing. When closed, it allows the ECM to pressurize or apply vacuum to the system to check for leaks. A stuck open or closed vent valve prevents proper EVAP system testing and operation.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive. May cause difficulty refueling if the vent valve is stuck closed.

causes.cause: Faulty EVAP vent solenoid stuck open or closed
Blocked vent filter on the charcoal canister
Wiring issue to the vent solenoid
Spider webs or debris blocking the vent tube

symptoms: Difficulty refueling (pump nozzle clicks off repeatedly)
Hissing sound when opening gas cap

fixSteps.title: Locate and Inspect Vent Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Find the vent valve, usually on or near the charcoal canister under the vehicle or near the fuel tank. Check the vent filter for blockage.

fixSteps.title: Test Vent Solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Check the vent solenoid resistance and verify it opens and closes when commanded. Apply 12V directly to test actuation.

fixSteps.title: Check Vent Tube
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the vent tube coming from the charcoal canister for blockages from debris, mud, or insects. Blow through the tube to check for flow.

fixSteps.title: Replace Vent Valve
fixSteps.instruction: If the vent solenoid is faulty, replace it. Some vehicles require canister replacement if the vent valve is integrated. Clear codes and verify.

faq.q: Where is the EVAP vent valve?
faq.a: The vent valve is typically located on or near the charcoal canister, often underneath the vehicle near the fuel tank area.

faq.q: Why is it hard to fill my gas tank with P0446?
faq.a: If the vent valve is stuck closed, the fuel tank cannot vent properly, causing back-pressure that trips the fuel pump nozzle off repeatedly.

faq.q: Can insects cause P0446?
faq.a: Yes, insects and spiders commonly nest in the vent tube opening, blocking airflow and triggering this code.

title: EVAP Vent Valve Circuit
shortDescription: The EVAP vent valve solenoid circuit has an electrical malfunction.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected an electrical fault in the EVAP vent valve solenoid circuit. This code specifically indicates a wiring or solenoid electrical issue rather than a mechanical problem with the vent system. The vent valve solenoid may have an open or shorted coil, or the wiring between the ECM and the solenoid may be damaged.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive. EVAP monitoring will be disabled but no safety concern.

causes.cause: Failed vent valve solenoid coil
Corroded or damaged wiring to vent valve
Poor electrical connection at vent valve connector
Water damage to vent valve due to its underbody location

fixSteps.title: Inspect Vent Valve and Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the vent valve and check the connector for corrosion or water damage. Due to its location, water intrusion is common.

fixSteps.title: Test Solenoid Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the vent valve solenoid resistance. Compare to specifications. An open or shorted coil requires replacement.

fixSteps.title: Check Circuit Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Verify power and ground at the vent valve connector. Trace wiring for damage, especially in areas exposed to road debris.

fixSteps.title: Replace Vent Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the vent valve solenoid if faulty. Ensure the new connector is properly sealed against moisture. Clear codes.

faq.q: Is P0449 common on GM trucks?
faq.a: Yes, P0449 is very common on GM trucks and SUVs due to the vent valve's exposed location near the fuel tank where it is prone to water and road debris damage.

faq.q: Can I replace the vent valve myself?
faq.a: Yes, the vent valve is usually accessible, though it may require raising the vehicle. It typically bolts to the charcoal canister with one electrical connector.

faq.q: Does P0449 affect gas mileage?
faq.a: No, P0449 does not affect fuel economy. It is purely an emissions control circuit issue.

title: EVAP Pressure Sensor Malfunction
shortDescription: The EVAP system pressure sensor is malfunctioning.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected a malfunction in the EVAP system pressure sensor (also called the fuel tank pressure sensor or FTP sensor). This sensor monitors the pressure in the fuel tank and EVAP system to detect leaks and control purge operations. A faulty sensor can provide erratic or out-of-range readings to the ECM.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive. EVAP monitoring will be compromised but vehicle operation is unaffected.

causes.cause: Faulty fuel tank pressure sensor
Wiring issue in the FTP sensor circuit
Corroded connector at the pressure sensor
Damaged sensor due to fuel contamination

fixSteps.title: Read Sensor Data
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to read the fuel tank pressure sensor value. With the gas cap off, it should read close to atmospheric pressure.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Sensor and Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the FTP sensor (usually on the fuel tank or fuel pump module) and check the connector for corrosion or damage.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor Circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Check the reference voltage (typically 5V) and ground at the sensor connector. Verify the signal wire is not shorted or open.

fixSteps.title: Replace FTP Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If the sensor readings are erratic or out of range, replace the fuel tank pressure sensor. Some are part of the fuel pump module.

faq.q: Where is the fuel tank pressure sensor?
faq.a: It is typically mounted on top of the fuel tank or integrated into the fuel pump module assembly.

faq.q: Can a bad FTP sensor cause starting problems?
faq.a: No, the FTP sensor is only used for EVAP system monitoring and does not affect engine starting or running.

faq.q: Is the FTP sensor the same as the fuel pressure sensor?
faq.a: No, the fuel tank pressure sensor monitors EVAP system pressure, while the fuel rail pressure sensor monitors fuel delivery pressure for injection.

title: EVAP Pressure Sensor Range
shortDescription: The EVAP pressure sensor reading is out of expected range or showing erratic behavior.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the fuel tank pressure sensor is providing readings outside the expected range or showing erratic voltage changes. This code specifically indicates a performance issue with the sensor rather than a complete circuit failure. Temperature changes, driving conditions, and fuel level should cause gradual pressure changes, and rapid or unexpected fluctuations trigger this code.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive. No impact on vehicle safety or performance.

causes.cause: Failing fuel tank pressure sensor with erratic output
Intermittent wiring connection to the sensor
Fuel tank overfilled causing abnormal pressure readings
Blocked or restricted sensor reference port

fixSteps.title: Monitor Sensor Live Data
fixSteps.instruction: Watch the FTP sensor readings on a scan tool during various conditions. Look for sudden jumps or erratic behavior.

fixSteps.title: Check Wiring Connections
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect and wiggle the FTP sensor connector while monitoring live data. Erratic readings during wiggle testing indicate a connection issue.

fixSteps.title: Check for Overfill Condition
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the tank is not overfilled. Excessive fuel can saturate the charcoal canister and cause abnormal pressure readings.

fixSteps.title: Replace FTP Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If the sensor is providing erratic readings with good wiring, replace the fuel tank pressure sensor. Clear codes and verify.

faq.q: Can overfilling the gas tank cause P0451?
faq.a: Yes, topping off the fuel tank past the first click of the nozzle can cause liquid fuel to enter the EVAP system and produce abnormal pressure readings.

faq.q: Is P0451 urgent?
faq.a: No, P0451 is not urgent. It is a low-severity emissions code that does not affect drivability or safety.

faq.q: Will P0451 cause other EVAP codes?
faq.a: A faulty FTP sensor can prevent proper EVAP leak testing, which may result in additional EVAP-related codes.

title: EVAP Pressure Sensor Low
shortDescription: The EVAP pressure sensor circuit voltage is below the expected minimum.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the fuel tank pressure sensor signal voltage is lower than the minimum expected threshold. This typically indicates a short to ground in the signal circuit, a failed sensor, or a disconnected reference voltage wire. The sensor should output a voltage proportional to the pressure in the fuel tank.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive. No impact on safety or engine performance.

causes.cause: Failed fuel tank pressure sensor
Short to ground in the sensor signal wire
Open or lost 5V reference voltage to the sensor
Corroded connector pins at the sensor

fixSteps.title: Check Sensor Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: With the key on and engine off, check the FTP sensor signal voltage. It should typically read between 0.5V and 4.5V.

fixSteps.title: Verify Reference Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Check that the 5V reference voltage is present at the sensor connector. A missing reference will cause low signal output.

fixSteps.title: Check for Short to Ground
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the sensor and check the signal wire for short to ground. No continuity should exist between the signal wire and chassis ground.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor or Repair Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the FTP sensor if it has failed, or repair any wiring faults found. Clear codes and verify.

faq.q: Can a bad ground cause P0452?
faq.a: A poor sensor ground can cause the signal voltage to read lower than expected, potentially triggering this code.

faq.q: Is the FTP sensor hard to replace?
faq.a: Difficulty varies by vehicle. On some vehicles it is accessible from underneath, while others require dropping the fuel tank.

faq.q: Will P0452 prevent EVAP leak testing?
faq.a: Yes, the ECM requires a functioning FTP sensor to perform EVAP leak detection tests.

title: EVAP Pressure Sensor High
shortDescription: The EVAP pressure sensor circuit voltage is above the expected maximum.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the fuel tank pressure sensor signal voltage is higher than the maximum expected threshold. This typically indicates an open signal circuit, a short to the reference voltage, or a failed sensor outputting maximum voltage. An open ground wire to the sensor can also cause this condition.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive. No safety or performance impact.

causes.cause: Failed fuel tank pressure sensor
Open circuit in the sensor signal wire
Signal wire shorted to the reference voltage wire
Open ground circuit to the sensor

fixSteps.title: Read Sensor Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Check the FTP sensor voltage on a scan tool. A reading near or at 5V suggests an open circuit or short to reference voltage.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Check the FTP sensor connector for spread or bent pins, corrosion, or disconnection that could create an open circuit.

fixSteps.title: Test Circuit Continuity
fixSteps.instruction: Check for continuity in the signal wire and ground wire from the sensor to the ECM. An open in either can cause high voltage.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor or Repair Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty FTP sensor or repair the open wiring. Clear codes and verify proper voltage readings.

faq.q: What does a high voltage FTP sensor reading mean?
faq.a: A voltage at or near 5V typically means the signal circuit is open (disconnected) and the sensor signal is being pulled up to the reference voltage.

faq.q: Can a disconnected sensor cause P0453?
faq.a: Yes, if the FTP sensor connector is disconnected, the signal line will float high, triggering P0453.

faq.q: Is P0453 related to fuel tank pressure issues?
faq.a: Not directly. P0453 is an electrical circuit issue with the sensor itself, not an indication of actual tank pressure problems.

title: EVAP System Large Leak
shortDescription: A large leak has been detected in the evaporative emission control system.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected a large leak in the EVAP system (typically greater than 0.040 inches in diameter). This is usually a straightforward problem such as a missing or very loose gas cap, a disconnected EVAP hose, or a severely cracked component. Large leaks are generally easier to find than the small leaks indicated by P0442.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive but fuel vapors are escaping into the atmosphere. Check the gas cap immediately.

causes.cause: Missing, loose, or damaged gas cap
Disconnected EVAP hose or vapor line
Cracked or damaged charcoal canister
Broken fuel tank filler neck

fixSteps.title: Check the Gas Cap
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the gas cap is present, properly tightened (click), and the seal is intact. Replace if damaged or missing.

fixSteps.title: Visual Inspection
fixSteps.instruction: Open the hood and check all visible EVAP hoses for disconnections or obvious damage. Check around the charcoal canister area under the vehicle.

fixSteps.title: Smoke Test
fixSteps.instruction: If visual inspection does not reveal the leak, perform an EVAP smoke test. Large leaks are usually visible quickly with smoke.

fixSteps.title: Repair the Leak
fixSteps.instruction: Reconnect any disconnected hoses or replace damaged components. Ensure all connections are secure. Clear codes and verify.

faq.q: Is a loose gas cap the most common cause?
faq.a: Yes, a loose, missing, or damaged gas cap is the most common cause of P0455 and should always be the first thing checked.

faq.q: How soon will the light go off after tightening the cap?
faq.a: The check engine light will not go off immediately. It may take several drive cycles (usually 2-3 days of normal driving) for the EVAP monitor to run and clear.

faq.q: Can I just clear the code?
faq.a: You can clear the code, but if the leak is still present it will return after the EVAP system runs its self-test during normal driving.

title: EVAP System Very Small Leak
shortDescription: A very small leak has been detected in the evaporative emission control system.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected a very small leak in the EVAP system (typically less than 0.020 inches in diameter). These are the smallest leaks the system can detect and are often the most difficult to locate. Common causes include aging gas cap seals, hairline cracks in EVAP hoses, or slight seepage from EVAP system seals and connections.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive. This is a very minor emissions concern with no safety impact.

causes.cause: Aging gas cap seal with minor deterioration
Hairline crack in an EVAP hose or connection
Slight leak at an EVAP component seal or O-ring
Marginal fit of aftermarket gas cap

fixSteps.title: Replace Gas Cap
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the gas cap with a new OEM-quality cap. Aftermarket caps may not seal as well. Clear codes and drive for 2-3 days.

fixSteps.title: Inspect EVAP Components
fixSteps.instruction: Carefully inspect all EVAP hoses, connections, and the charcoal canister for any signs of aging, cracking, or deterioration.

fixSteps.title: Perform Enhanced Smoke Test
fixSteps.instruction: Use an EVAP smoke machine with a very slow flow rate to detect the tiny leak. Pay close attention to connections and seals.
tools: EVAP smoke machine with fine adjustment

fixSteps.title: Repair Leak Source
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the leaking hose, seal, or connection identified during testing. Clear codes and allow several drive cycles for the monitor to complete.

faq.q: Should I try a new gas cap first?
faq.a: Yes, replacing the gas cap with an OEM cap is the cheapest and easiest first step. It resolves P0456 in a significant number of cases.

faq.q: Why is P0456 harder to fix than P0455?
faq.a: Very small leaks are much harder to locate because the leak rate is minimal. A sensitive smoke test or advanced EVAP test equipment may be needed.

faq.q: Can temperature changes cause P0456?
faq.a: Yes, temperature fluctuations can cause marginal seals to pass or fail the leak test intermittently, making diagnosis more challenging.

title: Fuel Level Sensor Circuit
shortDescription: The fuel level sensor circuit is malfunctioning.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected a malfunction in the fuel level sensor circuit. The fuel level sensor (fuel sending unit) is located inside the fuel tank and uses a float-operated variable resistor to send fuel level information to the instrument cluster and ECM. This code indicates the sensor signal is erratic, stuck, or outside the expected range.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive but fuel gauge readings will be unreliable, potentially leading to running out of fuel unexpectedly.

causes.cause: Failed fuel level sending unit with worn resistor card
Corroded wiring or connector at the fuel tank
Float stuck or detached from the sending unit arm
Open or short in the fuel level sensor circuit

symptoms: Fuel gauge reads incorrectly or erratically
Fuel gauge stuck on empty or full
Low fuel warning light comes on erroneously

fixSteps.title: Monitor Fuel Level Data
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to read the fuel level sensor value. Move the vehicle and check if the reading changes appropriately.

fixSteps.title: Check Wiring at Fuel Tank
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the fuel level sensor connector on top of the fuel tank for corrosion, damage, or loose connections.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the sensor and measure resistance while manually moving the float arm through its range. It should change smoothly from empty to full spec.

fixSteps.title: Replace Fuel Sending Unit
fixSteps.instruction: If the sensor is faulty, replace the fuel sending unit. This may require dropping the fuel tank or accessing through an access panel.

faq.q: Is it safe to drive with a broken fuel gauge?
faq.a: The vehicle is mechanically safe but you risk running out of fuel. Track your mileage and fill up regularly until the gauge is fixed.

faq.q: Where is the fuel level sensor?
faq.a: The fuel level sensor is inside the fuel tank, usually as part of the fuel pump module assembly.

faq.q: Can a bad fuel pump cause P0460?
faq.a: The fuel level sensor is often integrated into the fuel pump assembly, but P0460 specifically relates to the level sensor circuit, not the pump itself.

title: Fuel Level Sensor Range
shortDescription: The fuel level sensor signal is out of the expected range or showing irrational behavior.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the fuel level sensor is providing readings that do not correspond to expected fuel level changes. The ECM monitors fuel level over time and compares it to fuel consumption. If the fuel level does not change as expected based on engine fuel usage, this code is set. This can indicate a stuck float, worn sensor, or intermittent circuit issue.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive but fuel gauge will be unreliable. Monitor fuel consumption manually.

causes.cause: Fuel level sensor float stuck in one position
Worn resistor card on the sending unit
Bent or binding float arm
Fuel tank deformed restricting float movement

symptoms: Fuel gauge reads the same level regardless of actual fuel

fixSteps.title: Observe Fuel Level Over Time
fixSteps.instruction: Monitor fuel level sensor readings over several fill-ups and driving sessions. A stuck or irrational reading confirms the issue.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Float and Arm
fixSteps.instruction: If accessible, check the fuel sending unit float and arm for binding, bending, or debris preventing free movement.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor Through Range
fixSteps.instruction: Manually move the float arm and verify the resistance changes smoothly through the entire range without dead spots.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sending Unit
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the fuel sending unit if the float is stuck or the resistor card is worn. Clear codes after replacement.

faq.q: Can debris in the fuel tank cause P0461?
faq.a: Yes, sediment or debris can interfere with the float or float arm, preventing it from moving freely and accurately representing the fuel level.

faq.q: Is the fuel level sensor part of the fuel pump?
faq.a: On most modern vehicles, the fuel level sensor is integrated into the fuel pump module assembly, though it can sometimes be replaced separately.

faq.q: How does the ECM know the fuel level is wrong?
faq.a: The ECM tracks fuel consumption based on injector operation and compares it to fuel level changes. If consumption does not match level changes, the code is set.

title: Fuel Level Sensor Low Input
shortDescription: The fuel level sensor circuit signal is lower than expected.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the fuel level sensor signal voltage or resistance is below the minimum expected value. This typically indicates the sensor is reading as if the tank is always empty or the signal wire has a short to ground. On many vehicles, low resistance in the fuel level circuit corresponds to a full reading, so interpretation depends on the specific vehicle.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive but fuel gauge readings will be incorrect.

causes.cause: Failed fuel level sensor with shorted resistor element
Signal wire shorted to ground
Corroded connector causing low resistance path
Float sunk to bottom due to fuel saturation

symptoms: Fuel gauge stuck on empty or full (depending on circuit design)

fixSteps.title: Check Sensor Reading
fixSteps.instruction: Read the fuel level sensor value with a scan tool and compare to actual fuel level. A reading stuck at minimum indicates a low input fault.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Wiring for Short
fixSteps.instruction: Check the fuel level sensor wiring from the connector to the ECM for chafing or shorts to ground.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the sensor and measure resistance. Compare to manufacturer specifications for empty, half, and full positions.

fixSteps.title: Replace Fuel Sending Unit
fixSteps.instruction: If the sensor resistance is incorrect, replace the fuel sending unit assembly. Clear codes and verify gauge operation.

faq.q: Does low input mean the gauge reads empty?
faq.a: Not necessarily. On some vehicles low resistance means full, while on others it means empty. Check your vehicle's specific fuel level sensor specifications.

faq.q: Can I test the fuel level sensor without dropping the tank?
faq.a: You can test the circuit at the connector on top of the fuel tank on vehicles with an access panel. Some vehicles require fuel tank removal.

faq.q: Is it just the sensor or could it be the gauge?
faq.a: P0462 specifically points to the sensor circuit signal to the ECM. The gauge is driven by the ECM or body control module, so the gauge itself is rarely the problem.

title: Fuel Level Sensor High Input
shortDescription: The fuel level sensor circuit signal is higher than expected.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the fuel level sensor signal voltage or resistance is above the maximum expected value. This typically indicates an open circuit in the sensor or wiring, or a sensor that has failed with maximum resistance. The fuel gauge may read incorrectly as a result.
safetyImpact: Safe to drive but fuel gauge readings will be unreliable.

causes.cause: Open circuit in the fuel level sensor
Disconnected or broken wire to the fuel level sensor
Corroded connector with open contact
Failed fuel level sensor with open resistor

symptoms: Fuel gauge stuck on full or empty (depending on circuit design)
Fuel gauge not responding to actual fuel level changes

fixSteps.title: Read Sensor Data
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to check the fuel level sensor signal. A maximum or out-of-range reading indicates a high input condition.

fixSteps.title: Check Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the fuel level sensor connector for open pins, corrosion, or disconnection. Clean and secure all connections.

fixSteps.title: Test Circuit Continuity
fixSteps.instruction: Check for continuity in the signal and ground wires from the sensor to the ECM. An open circuit in either wire will cause a high input.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor or Repair Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: If the sensor has an open resistor element, replace the fuel sending unit. Repair any broken wiring. Clear codes and verify.

faq.q: Can a disconnected sensor connector cause P0463?
faq.a: Yes, a disconnected or loose connector is one of the most common causes of the high input reading since it creates an open circuit.

faq.q: Is P0463 related to the fuel pump?
faq.a: Not directly, but the fuel level sensor is often part of the fuel pump module, so replacing the sensor may involve the fuel pump assembly.

faq.q: Will P0463 affect my fuel economy calculations?
faq.a: Your onboard fuel economy calculations and distance-to-empty readings will be inaccurate with a faulty fuel level sensor.

title: Cooling Fan 1 Control Circuit
shortDescription: The engine cooling fan 1 control circuit has a malfunction.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected a fault in the cooling fan 1 control circuit. The engine cooling fan is critical for maintaining proper engine temperature, especially at idle and low speeds. This code may indicate a failed fan motor, faulty relay, blown fuse, or wiring problem in the fan circuit. Some vehicles use a separate fan control module that can also fail.
safetyImpact: Risk of engine overheating, especially in traffic or at idle. Do not drive in stop-and-go conditions until repaired.

causes.cause: Failed cooling fan motor
Faulty cooling fan relay
Blown cooling fan fuse
Wiring issue in the fan circuit

symptoms: Engine overheating at idle or low speeds
Cooling fan does not run when engine is hot

fixSteps.title: Check Fan Fuse and Relay
fixSteps.instruction: Check the cooling fan fuse in the fuse box. Swap the fan relay with a similar relay to test. A blown fuse may indicate a short.

fixSteps.title: Test Fan Motor Directly
fixSteps.instruction: Connect the fan motor directly to 12V battery power to verify the motor runs. A seized motor draws excessive current and blows fuses.

fixSteps.title: Check Wiring and Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the fan motor connector and wiring for damage, corrosion, or breaks. Check the ground connection.

fixSteps.title: Replace Failed Component
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the fan motor, relay, or repair wiring as needed. Clear codes and verify the fan operates when the engine reaches operating temperature.

faq.q: Can I drive with the cooling fan not working?
faq.a: You can drive at highway speeds where airflow cools the radiator, but avoid stop-and-go traffic where the engine will overheat without the fan.

faq.q: How do I know if the fan motor or relay is bad?
faq.a: If the fan runs when powered directly with jumper wires, the motor is good and the relay or control circuit is the problem.

faq.q: Can a bad temperature sensor cause P0480?
faq.a: A bad temperature sensor would prevent the fan from being commanded on, but P0480 specifically indicates a circuit fault rather than a command issue.

title: Vehicle Speed Sensor Malfunction
shortDescription: The vehicle speed sensor (VSS) signal is not being received by the ECM.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that no vehicle speed signal is being received or the signal is erratic. The vehicle speed sensor provides speed information used for shift points, cruise control, speedometer operation, and ABS functions. The sensor is typically located on the transmission output shaft or rear differential. A failed sensor, damaged tone ring, or wiring issue can cause this code.
safetyImpact: Speedometer may not function. Transmission shift quality may be affected. ABS and cruise control may be disabled.

causes.cause: Failed vehicle speed sensor
Damaged or contaminated sensor tone ring or reluctor
Wiring issue between the sensor and ECM
Corroded connector at the speed sensor

symptoms: Transmission shifting issues (harsh or delayed shifts)
Cruise control and ABS not functioning

fixSteps.title: Test Speed Sensor Signal
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to read the vehicle speed while driving. If the reading is zero or erratic while the vehicle is moving, the sensor or circuit is faulty.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Speed Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the speed sensor on the transmission or differential. Check for metal debris on the sensor tip and inspect the connector.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor Output
fixSteps.instruction: With the sensor connected, check for an AC voltage signal while spinning the drive wheels by hand (vehicle safely raised).

fixSteps.title: Replace Speed Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If the sensor is faulty, replace it. Clean any metal debris from the mounting area. Clear codes and test drive.

faq.q: Can I drive without a working speed sensor?
faq.a: The vehicle will run but the speedometer, cruise control, and ABS may not function. Transmission shifting may also be erratic.

faq.q: Where is the vehicle speed sensor located?
faq.a: Typically on the transmission tailshaft housing or output shaft area. On some vehicles, the ABS wheel speed sensors provide the speed signal to the ECM.

faq.q: Can a speed sensor affect fuel economy?
faq.a: Yes, the ECM uses vehicle speed data for fuel management decisions, so a faulty sensor can slightly impact fuel economy and shift timing.

title: Vehicle Speed Sensor Range
shortDescription: The vehicle speed sensor signal is out of the expected range or showing irrational data.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the vehicle speed sensor is producing a signal that does not correlate with expected conditions. For example, the sensor may show high speed while the engine RPM is at idle, or show zero speed when RPM and other data indicate the vehicle is moving. This code indicates a performance or rationality issue rather than a complete signal loss.
safetyImpact: Speedometer readings may be inaccurate. Transmission and cruise control functions may be affected.

causes.cause: Faulty vehicle speed sensor producing erratic signal
Damaged tone ring with missing teeth
Incorrect tire size affecting speed calculation
Intermittent wiring connection

symptoms: Cruise control surging or not engaging

fixSteps.title: Compare Speed Sources
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to compare the VSS reading with GPS speed or a known speedometer. Note any discrepancies.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Tone Ring
fixSteps.instruction: If accessible, inspect the speed sensor tone ring or reluctor wheel for damage, missing teeth, or excessive rust.

fixSteps.title: Check Sensor Air Gap
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the speed sensor air gap to the tone ring is within specification. Too large a gap can cause erratic signals.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor or Tone Ring
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the speed sensor or damaged tone ring. Clear codes and verify accurate speed readings during a test drive.

faq.q: Can changing tire sizes cause P0501?
faq.a: Yes, significantly different tire sizes can cause the speed calculation to be outside the expected range, potentially triggering this code.

faq.q: What is a tone ring?
faq.a: A tone ring (reluctor wheel) is a toothed wheel that spins with the axle or transmission output shaft. The speed sensor reads the teeth passing by to calculate speed.

faq.q: Can a bad ABS sensor cause P0501?
faq.a: On vehicles that derive speed from ABS sensors, a faulty wheel speed sensor can provide incorrect data that triggers this code.

title: Vehicle Speed Sensor Low
shortDescription: The vehicle speed sensor circuit is reading lower than expected.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the vehicle speed sensor signal is lower than expected for the current operating conditions. This could mean the signal is absent, intermittent, or stuck at a low level when the vehicle should be showing movement based on engine RPM and gear selection data.
safetyImpact: Speedometer may not register or read low. Transmission shift points may be delayed.

causes.cause: Faulty vehicle speed sensor
Open or high resistance in the VSS signal circuit
Excessive air gap between sensor and tone ring
Corroded or loose sensor connector

symptoms: Delayed or no upshifts in transmission

fixSteps.title: Check VSS Signal
fixSteps.instruction: Drive the vehicle while monitoring VSS data on a scan tool. If the reading is zero or very low while driving, the sensor or circuit is the issue.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Sensor and Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Check the speed sensor connector for corrosion and proper seating. Clean with electrical contact cleaner.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor Output
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the AC voltage output from the sensor while spinning a drive wheel by hand. A good sensor produces a measurable signal.

fixSteps.title: Replace Speed Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the speed sensor if output is absent or low. Ensure proper installation depth and connector seating.

faq.q: Can a bad speed sensor cause the transmission not to shift?
faq.a: Yes, the transmission uses vehicle speed data to determine shift points. Without it, the transmission may stay in a lower gear.

faq.q: Is the speed sensor easy to access?
faq.a: On most vehicles, the speed sensor screws into the transmission housing and is accessible from underneath the vehicle.

faq.q: Can metal shavings affect the speed sensor?
faq.a: Yes, magnetic speed sensors can attract metal debris from normal transmission wear, which can interfere with accurate signal generation.

title: Vehicle Speed Sensor High/Erratic
shortDescription: The vehicle speed sensor is providing intermittent, erratic, or high-speed signals.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the vehicle speed sensor signal is intermittent, erratic, or reading higher than expected. This can cause the speedometer to spike or jump around. The ECM cross-references speed data with engine RPM and wheel speed sensor data to detect this condition. Common causes include a loose connector, damaged wiring, or a failing sensor generating noise.
safetyImpact: Erratic speedometer readings and unpredictable transmission shifts. ABS and stability control may be affected.

causes.cause: Loose or intermittent speed sensor connector
Damaged wiring generating electrical noise
Failing speed sensor generating erratic signals
Electromagnetic interference from aftermarket electronics

symptoms: ABS or traction control warning lights

fixSteps.title: Monitor Speed Data
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to graph the VSS signal while driving. Look for sudden spikes or drops that do not correlate with actual speed changes.

fixSteps.title: Check Connector and Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Wiggle test the speed sensor connector and wiring while monitoring the signal. An intermittent connection will show signal dropouts.

fixSteps.title: Inspect for Interference
fixSteps.instruction: Check if aftermarket electronics, amplifiers, or wiring are routed near the VSS wiring that could cause electromagnetic interference.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor and Repair Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the speed sensor if it is generating noise. Repair or reroute any damaged wiring. Clear codes and verify.

faq.q: Can a loose connector cause erratic speed readings?
faq.a: Yes, a loose or corroded connector is one of the most common causes of intermittent speed sensor signals.

faq.q: Why does my speedometer jump around?
faq.a: An erratic speed sensor signal causes the speedometer to jump because it displays whatever speed data it receives from the ECM.

faq.q: Can aftermarket accessories cause P0503?
faq.a: In rare cases, aftermarket amplifiers or electronics with poor shielding can introduce electrical noise into nearby sensor circuits.

title: Idle Air Control Malfunction
shortDescription: The idle air control (IAC) system is not functioning properly.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the idle air control system cannot maintain the target idle speed. The IAC valve or electronic throttle body controls airflow at idle to maintain a stable RPM. This code can be triggered by a stuck IAC valve, carbon buildup in the throttle body, vacuum leaks, or a faulty IAC motor. On drive-by-wire vehicles, this may relate to the electronic throttle body.
safetyImpact: Engine may stall unexpectedly, particularly when coming to a stop or in gear. Use caution in traffic.

causes.cause: Carbon buildup on IAC valve or throttle body
Faulty IAC valve motor or stepper motor
Vacuum leak affecting idle airflow
Wiring issue in the IAC control circuit

symptoms: Idle too high or too low

fixSteps.title: Clean Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the air intake hose and clean the throttle body bore and blade with throttle body cleaner. Do not use carb cleaner on electronic throttles.

fixSteps.title: Clean or Replace IAC Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the IAC valve and clean the pintle and port with cleaner. If the valve is stuck, soak it in solvent. Replace if cleaning does not help.

fixSteps.title: Check for Vacuum Leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect all vacuum hoses and intake gaskets for leaks. A vacuum leak introduces unmetered air that affects idle control.
tools: propane enrichment tool or smoke machine

fixSteps.title: Relearn Idle
fixSteps.instruction: After cleaning or replacing components, perform an idle relearn procedure per your vehicle's service manual. Clear codes and verify stable idle.

faq.q: What is the idle air control valve?
faq.a: The IAC valve is a motorized valve that controls the amount of air bypassing the throttle plate to maintain a stable idle speed regardless of engine load.

faq.q: Can cleaning fix P0505?
faq.a: Often yes. Carbon buildup is the most common cause, and a thorough cleaning of the IAC valve and throttle body resolves many P0505 codes.

faq.q: Do I need to do an idle relearn after cleaning?
faq.a: Many vehicles require an idle relearn procedure after cleaning the throttle body or replacing the IAC valve. Check your service manual for the specific procedure.

title: Idle Speed Lower Than Expected
shortDescription: The engine idle speed is lower than the ECM's target RPM.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the engine idle speed is consistently lower than the programmed target. The ECM is attempting to raise the idle speed but cannot achieve the desired RPM. This can be caused by restricted airflow at idle, a sticking IAC valve, carbon-restricted throttle body, or excessive engine load from accessories.
safetyImpact: Engine may stall when coming to a stop or when additional loads are applied (AC, power steering).

causes.cause: Carbon buildup restricting throttle body or IAC passage
IAC valve not opening enough due to sticking or failure
Vacuum leak causing lean condition at idle
Excessive parasitic load from AC compressor or alternator

symptoms: Idle speed noticeably low (below 600 RPM typically)
Engine shaking or vibrating at idle
Stalling when shifting into gear or turning on AC

fixSteps.title: Clean Throttle Body and IAC
fixSteps.instruction: Thoroughly clean the throttle body bore, plate, and IAC passages with appropriate cleaner to restore proper airflow.

fixSteps.title: Check for Vacuum Leaks
fixSteps.instruction: With the engine idling, spray carburetor cleaner around intake gaskets and vacuum connections. RPM increase indicates a leak at that point.

fixSteps.title: Test IAC Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to command the IAC valve to different positions and verify the idle RPM changes accordingly.
tools: OBD-II scan tool with bidirectional control

fixSteps.title: Perform Idle Relearn
fixSteps.instruction: After cleaning, perform the idle relearn procedure. Allow the engine to reach operating temperature and idle with all accessories off.

faq.q: Why does my engine stall when I turn on the AC?
faq.a: The AC compressor adds a significant load to the engine. If the idle control system cannot compensate by increasing airflow, the idle drops too low and the engine stalls.

faq.q: Is low idle speed dangerous?
faq.a: Low idle speed itself is not dangerous, but unexpected stalling in traffic can be a safety hazard.

faq.q: Can a dirty air filter cause P0506?
faq.a: While a severely restricted air filter can reduce airflow, it is rarely the sole cause of P0506. The throttle body and IAC passages are more common culprits.

title: Idle Speed Higher Than Expected
shortDescription: The engine idle speed is higher than the ECM's target RPM.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected that the engine idle speed is consistently higher than the programmed target. The ECM is attempting to lower the idle speed but cannot achieve the desired RPM. This typically indicates unmetered air entering the engine through a vacuum leak, stuck-open IAC valve, or air intake system leak.
safetyImpact: High idle can cause the vehicle to lurch forward when shifting into gear from park. Use caution.

causes.cause: Vacuum leak at intake manifold gasket or hose
IAC valve stuck open
Throttle body not closing fully
EVAP purge valve stuck open allowing extra air

symptoms: Idle speed noticeably high (above 900-1000 RPM typically)
Vehicle surging or lurching when shifting into gear

fixSteps.title: Check for Vacuum Leaks
fixSteps.instruction: With the engine idling, carefully listen for hissing sounds and inspect all vacuum hoses and intake connections. Use smoke or propane to pinpoint leaks.
tools: smoke machine or propane enrichment tool

fixSteps.title: Inspect Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the throttle plate closes completely. Check for carbon deposits preventing full closure. Clean if necessary.

fixSteps.title: Test IAC Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Command the IAC valve closed using a scan tool. If idle speed does not drop, the valve may be stuck open or there is an air leak.
tools: OBD-II scan tool with bidirectional control

fixSteps.title: Repair Leaks and Relearn
fixSteps.instruction: Fix any vacuum leaks found and replace the stuck IAC valve if needed. Perform idle relearn procedure. Clear codes.

faq.q: Can a vacuum leak cause high idle?
faq.a: Yes, vacuum leaks are the number one cause of high idle speed because unmetered air enters the engine, causing the ECM to lose idle control.

faq.q: What is a normal idle speed?
faq.a: Most engines idle between 600-900 RPM when warmed up. Cold idle may be higher at 1000-1200 RPM until the engine warms up.

faq.q: Can a stuck throttle cable cause P0507?
faq.a: On vehicles with cable-operated throttles, a sticky or adjusted throttle cable can hold the throttle slightly open, causing high idle.

title: Closed Throttle Position Switch
shortDescription: The closed throttle position switch circuit is malfunctioning.
fullDescription: The ECM has detected a problem with the closed throttle position switch or the throttle position sensor indicating closed throttle. This switch tells the ECM when the throttle is fully closed so it can control idle speed properly. On electronic throttle systems, this refers to the TPS reading at the closed position being outside the expected range.
safetyImpact: Idle control may be affected, potentially causing stalling or high idle.

causes.cause: Misadjusted or failed throttle position sensor
Throttle body not returning to fully closed position
Faulty closed throttle position switch
Wiring issue in the TPS circuit

fixSteps.title: Check TPS Reading at Idle
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to read the TPS percentage at idle. It should read close to 0% (typically 0-3%) when the throttle is fully closed.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Throttle Mechanism
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the throttle plate fully closes when released. Check for binding in the throttle linkage or cable.

fixSteps.title: Test TPS Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Check TPS signal voltage at closed throttle (typically 0.4-0.8V). If out of range, adjust or replace the TPS.

fixSteps.title: Replace TPS or Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: If the TPS cannot be adjusted to spec, replace the TPS or throttle body assembly. Perform throttle relearn after replacement.

faq.q: What is the closed throttle position switch?
faq.a: Some older vehicles have a separate switch that signals the ECM when the throttle is fully closed. Modern vehicles use the TPS to determine closed throttle position.
